blocks.content: At some point Ethan and Cassandra had drifted to the edge of the water.

Not all the way in — just to where the sand was wet and the tide came up to meet them and then pulled back, slow and regular, like breathing. They were standing close enough that their arms touched and far enough from the party that the music had become something ambient, a warmth in the background rather than a presence.
Behind them, the fire. The tiki torches. The voices of the people they loved.
In front of them: the water, and the dark, and the absolute open expanse of what came next.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I keep needing to say it.
He understood. Some things you had to say more than once before the weight of them fully landed.
She leaned into his side. He put his arm around her. The ocean did what it does.
She tilted her head to look at him.

She pressed her mouth together and looked back at the water. The tide came in and pulled out again. Somewhere behind them, Nathan laughed at something — that particular register he only hit when something had genuinely surprised him — and then, faintly, Maya's voice, the shorter real laugh she almost never gave.
She turned to look at him. He had the expression of someone who had known about this for longer than tonight and had been waiting patiently for her to notice.
She looked back toward the fire.

She didn't say anything for a moment. Then something in her face settled — the particular settling of someone who has been worried about a person for a long time and has just been given a small permission to stop.
